core_takeaway: Agent_1 beat Agent_5 because the first turn anchored Quill as Agent_1's protected item and the group later converted that anchor into the stability baseline. Agent_5, despite privately valuing Quill most, publicly defended Jewel and accepted the baseline, so the last position held a much lower-value item.
why_or_counterexample: Yes. The process shows more than a numeric win: Agent_1 used the opening to claim Quill, later speakers treated Quill-to-Agent_1 as core, and even Agent_5's unanimous final proposal preserved that allocation. The counterweight is that Agent_1 could not also force Stone; coalition vetoes limited the advantage to the initial Quill anchor.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Agent_1 opened by naming Quill as its top item and proposing Quill to itself. The discussion quickly normalized Quill-to-Agent_1 while Agent_3's Stone/Jewel demands were treated as destabilizing and Agent_5 focused on keeping Jewel.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Private reasoning confirms Agent_1's plan was to lock the consensus while holding Quill. Agent_5 privately listed Quill as its top priority but still planned to protect Jewel and preserve the baseline.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: Round 1 included competing high-payoff proposals and failed unanimity. By Round 2 all five formal proposals were identical to the baseline: Quill to Agent_1, Apple to Agent_2, Pencil to Agent_3, Stone to Agent_4, Jewel to Agent_5.
phase_notes.private_voting: In Round 1 Agent_1 and Agent_2 rejected the baseline to pursue higher immediate utility, while Agents 3-5 backed it. In Round 2 every agent accepted every identical baseline proposal; Agent_1 accepted because Quill maximized its utility, Agent_5 because Jewel stayed with it.
phase_notes.reflection: Reflections codified the stability story: the baseline protected top items for most agents, Agent_3's Stone/Jewel desire was the main friction, and any future changes needed broad consent without disturbing Quill, Stone, or Jewel.

mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1 used the opening position to establish Quill as its own must-have item. That claim became the starting point for later bargaining, even though Agent_5 actually valued Quill more.

evidence.quote: as long as Quill remains mine

mechanisms.explanation: Other speakers repeatedly treated Quill-to-Agent_1 as a fixed point and framed deviations around Stone or Jewel as the real threats to consensus. This insulated Agent_1's claim while later speakers bargained around it.

evidence.quote: clear alignment pattern: Quill with Agent_1

mechanisms.theme: Last speaker underclaimed its true top item
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_5 publicly defended Jewel and later voted for the baseline, while private thinking shows Quill was actually Agent_5's highest-value item. This left the last position with only Jewel's discounted utility.

evidence.quote: I want to keep Jewel with me

mechanisms.theme: Final proposal was last-position procedurally, but first-anchored substantively
mechanisms.explanation: The unanimously accepted proposal was submitted by Agent_5, yet its content still assigned Quill to Agent_1. This shows that formal last-mover proposal status did not translate into a better outcome for Agent_5.

mechanisms.theme: Coalition veto limited the first-position advantage
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1 tried to pursue an even better Quill-plus-Stone outcome in Round 1, but that proposal failed. The first position helped Agent_1 keep Quill, not capture every high-value item.

evidence.quote: Proposal #2 shows a high payoff for Agent_1

mechanisms.theme: Reflection converted the anchor into a stability rule
mechanisms.explanation: The reflection phase reframed the baseline as the path to consensus and described changes to Stone or Jewel as destabilizing. That post hoc learning reinforced the original Quill-to-Agent_1 allocation.

evidence.quote: Core alignment exists around the baseline
any change that takes Stone away from Agent_4
when the top-item protections are respected
